results.possibleIdentity: Stan VanDerBeek (1927–1984) was a prominent American experimental filmmaker, animator, and computer artist who was a key figure in the integration of art and technology during the 1960s and 1970s. He is widely recognized as a pioneer of 'expanded cinema' and multimedia installations. After studying at Cooper Union and Black Mountain College, VanDerBeek became a central figure in the New York avant-garde scene, collaborating with artists like Merce Cunningham, John Cage, and Claes Oldenburg. He was deeply involved with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), collaborating with Bell Labs engineer Kenneth C. Knowlton on early computer-generated films, such as the 'Poemfield' series (1966–1971) and a project for E.A.T. titled 'Man and His World.' His most famous physical work was the 'Movie Drome' (1963–1965), a geodesic dome in Stony Point, New York, used for immersive multi-screen projections. In 1970, he became the first artist-in-residence at the MIT Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS), where he explored telecommunications art, including transmitting images via fax and developing interactive television broadcasts like 'Violence Sonata.'

results.possibleIdentity: Susan (Sue) Hartnett is an American artist who was a key figure in the early operations of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Born in 1940, she served as the organization's administrative coordinator starting in 1967, managing mail, requests, and daily operations alongside founders Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg. Beyond her administrative role, she was a participant in the foundational E.A.T. event '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' in 1966, notably performing in Steve Paxton's piece 'Physical Things.' During this performance, the artist Elaine Sturtevant painted Hartnett’s face and hands with 'Spectratherm' (a liquid crystal material sensitive to body temperature). As an artist in her own right, Hartnett is recognized for her large-scale charcoal drawings and pastels that observe the rhythmic movements of nature, particularly field and dune grasses. Her work is held in the collections of the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Fogg Art Museum at Harvard University, and the Museum of Contemporary Art, Los Angeles.

Susan (Sue) Weil (born 1930) is an American artist who collaborated closely with Robert Rauschenberg, a co-founder of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), during the early stages of his experimental career. In the late 1940s and early 1950s, she and Rauschenberg (then her husband) created a series of blueprint photograms, such as 'Female Figure' (ca. 1950), which are considered early milestones in the intersection of art and experimental processes. While her most famous direct collaborations with Rauschenberg occurred before the formal founding of E.A.T. in 1966, she remained an active artist in the New York art scene throughout the late 1960s and 1970s. Her work often involves multi-dimensional canvases and fragmented imagery, and she is frequently referred to as 'Sue' in historical accounts of Rauschenberg's circle and the experimental roots that led to the formation of E.A.T.

Susan (Sue) Stenger is a composer and musician who was associated with the group of artists and engineers gathered for the E.A.T. Pepsi Pavilion project at Expo '70 in Osaka. In documentation regarding the live programming of the Pavilion, she is mentioned as 'Sue' (referenced as part of the 'Albany group') who contributed ideas for the naming and conceptualization of elements within the experimental environment. Active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, Stenger later became known for her cross-disciplinary work in experimental music and performance, often collaborating with figures from the same avant-garde circles that formed E.A.T.

results.possibleIdentity: Ronald Chase (1934–2025) was a prominent American multidisciplinary artist, filmmaker, and opera designer based in San Francisco. He is widely credited as a pioneer in the integration of film and projection technology within live operatic and theatrical performances, a technique he began developing in the late 1960s. After studying dance and design at Bard College and touring with the José Limón Dance Company, Chase moved to San Francisco in 1964. His experimental film career began with the short 'Fragments' (1965), and he gained significant recognition for his 1969 films 'Clown' and 'Chameleon.' In 1968, he revolutionized opera staging by using film projections on scrims for a production of Benjamin Britten's 'The Turn of the Screw' at the Washington Opera, followed by a long-term collaboration with director Frank Corsaro on productions such as 'Lulu' and 'Koanga.' His work often explored the intersection of visual arts and technology, aligning with the experimental spirit of the era; notably, he collaborated with experimental composer Pauline Oliveros in the early 1970s. Chase also documented significant cultural moments, including filming San Francisco's first Gay Pride Parade in 1972. In 1993, he founded the San Francisco Art & Film Program to provide arts education to teenagers. His photographic and visual works are held in the collections of the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, and the George Eastman House.

results.possibleIdentity: Clark Coolidge (born 1939) is an American experimental poet, musician, and editor who became a prominent figure in the New York School and the Language poetry movement. Active since the early 1960s, he is known for a 'plethoric' and 'material' approach to language, treating words as physical objects or geological sediments—a style influenced by his background in geology and his career as a jazz drummer. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was deeply involved in the avant-garde scene, collaborating with various artists, musicians, and engineers. Notably, his work was featured in the 1968 Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) catalogue, where he contributed an audio collage of political speeches, noise, and static. During this period, he was a member of the psychedelic band Serpent Power (1967) and later collaborated with the composer Alvin Curran and the visual artist Philip Guston (on the book 'Baffling Means'). His poetic works from this era, such as 'Flag Flutter & U.S. Electric' (1966) and 'Space' (1970), are celebrated for their experimental syntax and focus on the sonic and visual qualities of words. He also conducted tape experiments and produced the radio program 'Words' for KPFA in 1969.

results.possibleIdentity: Lotte Cherin (likely the artist referred to as Lotte Chern) is a Southern California-based artist, metalsmith, and arts administrator who has been active since at least the late 1960s. She is widely recognized as the copyright holder and representative for the estate of Ruth White (1925–2013), a pioneering electronic music composer known for her early use of the Moog synthesizer in the late 1960s and early 1970s. This connection aligns with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement's focus on bridging art and emerging technologies. Cherin's own artistic career is documented in the archival records of the Woman's Building in Los Angeles, where she has an artist file covering the years 1980–1985 and is included in lists of artists active from 1960 to 1990. She was also featured as an artist and metalsmith in the 1995 'ALCHEMY' exhibition at Cabrillo College. Professionally, she served as a Senior Client Technologies Analyst and Manager at Pepperdine University between 2002 and 2015. She currently serves as the Chair of the Malibu Arts Commission, a role she has held for several years, where she advocates for public art and cultural programming in Malibu, California.
